title: Universal Declaration of Human Rights 1948
source_name: Universal Declaration of Human Rights
text: The UDHR was adopted by the UN General Assembly on 10 December 1948 with 48 votes for, none against, and 8 abstentions. While non-binding as a GA resolution, it has attained the status of customary international law in substantial part (Filartiga v. Pena-Irala, US 2nd Cir. 1980). Key provisions: Article 1 (all human beings born free and equal in dignity); Article 3 (right to life, liberty and security); Article 5 (prohibition of torture and cruel treatment); Article 7 (equality before the law); Article 9 (no arbitrary arrest); Article 10 (fair trial); Article 12 (privacy); Article 18 (freedom of thought and religion); Article 19 (freedom of opinion and expression); Article 20 (freedom of association); Article 21 (democratic participation); Article 25 (adequate standard of living). The UDHR formed the basis for the twin Covenants (ICCPR and ICESCR) adopted in 1966.

title: International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights 1966 — Core Provisions
source_name: International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights
text: The ICCPR entered into force in 1976 with 173 States Parties. It protects civil and political rights with a horizontal treaty body (Human Rights Committee) to supervise implementation. Article 6 protects the right to life; no one shall be arbitrarily deprived of life. Article 7 prohibits torture, cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment. Article 9 protects liberty and security of person. Article 14 guarantees the right to a fair trial including equality before courts, presumption of innocence, legal assistance, and right of appeal. Article 17 protects privacy, family, home, and correspondence. Article 18 protects freedom of thought, conscience and religion. Article 19 protects freedom of expression. Under General Comment 36 (2018), the right to life extends to threats from climate change, armed conflict, and corporate environmental harm. States must implement treaty obligations domestically through legislation, judicial decisions, and administrative measures.

title: ICCPR — Non-Derogable Rights and Derogation Clause
source_name: ICCPR Article 4 — Derogation
citation: ICCPR Art. 4, HRC General Comment No. 29 (2001)
text: ICCPR Article 4 allows States to derogate from their obligations in time of public emergency that threatens the life of the nation, provided derogations are strictly required by the exigencies of the situation, non-discriminatory, and notified through the UN Secretary-General. Article 4(2) lists non-derogable rights from which no derogation is ever permitted: right to life (Art. 6), freedom from torture (Art. 7), prohibition of slavery (Art. 8(1)(2)), no imprisonment for inability to fulfil a contractual obligation (Art. 11), no conviction for retroactive criminal laws (Art. 15), recognition as a person before the law (Art. 16), and freedom of thought, conscience and religion (Art. 18). HRC General Comment 29 (2001) elaborates that some additional rights may be non-derogable under customary law even if not listed in Article 4(2).

title: International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights 1966
source_name: International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights
text: The ICESCR entered into force in 1976 with 171 States Parties. It protects economic, social and cultural rights subject to progressive realisation to the maximum of available resources. Article 2(1) imposes the obligation of progressive realisation but also requires immediate non-discrimination obligations. Article 6 protects the right to work. Article 7 protects just and favourable conditions of work including equal pay. Article 9 protects the right to social security. Article 11 protects the right to an adequate standard of living including food, clothing and housing. Article 12 protects the right to the highest attainable standard of physical and mental health. Article 13 protects the right to education. CESCR General Comment 3 (1990) establishes minimum core obligations that must be met regardless of resource constraints. The Optional Protocol (2008) created an individual complaints mechanism.

title: Convention Against Torture 1984
source_name: Convention Against Torture and Other Cruel, Inhuman or Degrading Treatment or Punishment
text: The CAT entered into force in 1987 with 173 States Parties. Article 1 defines torture as severe pain or suffering inflicted by or at the instigation of or with the consent or acquiescence of a public official for purposes of obtaining information, punishing, intimidating, or discriminating. Article 2 requires States to take effective measures to prevent torture; no exceptional circumstances may be invoked to justify torture. Article 3 prohibits refoulement to a country where there are substantial grounds for believing the person would be subjected to torture. Article 16 prohibits cruel, inhuman and degrading treatment. The prohibition of torture is jus cogens and erga omnes (Prosecutor v. Furundzija, ICTY 1998). The Committee Against Torture monitors implementation and can receive individual complaints under Article 22. States must criminalise torture and establish jurisdiction including for acts committed abroad.

title: Convention on the Rights of the Child 1989
source_name: Convention on the Rights of the Child
text: The CRC is the most widely ratified human rights treaty with 196 States Parties (only the US has not ratified). It applies to every person under 18 unless national law provides majority earlier. Four guiding principles: non-discrimination (Art. 2); best interests of the child (Art. 3); right to life, survival and development (Art. 6); right to be heard (Art. 12). Key rights include protection from all forms of violence, abuse and exploitation (Art. 19), right to education (Art. 28), right to the highest attainable health standard (Art. 24), right to play and leisure (Art. 31), protection from economic exploitation and child labour (Art. 32), and protection from sexual exploitation (Art. 34). Optional Protocol on Children in Armed Conflict raises the age of direct participation in hostilities and compulsory recruitment to 18. Optional Protocol on Sale of Children criminalises sale, sexual exploitation and use in pornography.

title: Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women 1979
source_name: Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women
text: CEDAW, often called the 'international bill of rights for women', entered into force in 1981 with 189 States Parties. It defines discrimination against women as any distinction, exclusion or restriction based on sex that impairs or nullifies women's human rights. States must condemn all forms of discrimination and pursue a policy of eliminating it by all appropriate means. Article 7 requires equal political participation; Article 10 equal education; Article 11 equal employment; Article 12 equal healthcare; Article 14 addresses rural women; Articles 15-16 cover legal capacity and family law. CEDAW Committee General Recommendation 35 (2017) updated GR 19 on gender-based violence, confirming it as a form of discrimination. The Optional Protocol (1999) allows individual communications and inquiry procedures.

title: International Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination 1965
source_name: International Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination
text: CERD entered into force in 1969 with 182 States Parties. Article 1 defines racial discrimination broadly to include distinction, exclusion, restriction or preference based on race, colour, descent, national or ethnic origin. Article 2 requires States to condemn racial discrimination and pursue a policy of eliminating it. Article 4 requires criminalisation of hate speech and racially motivated violence. Article 5 prohibits racial discrimination in civil, political, economic, social and cultural rights. The CERD Committee has developed an 'urgent action and early warning' procedure. Under General Recommendation XIV, differential treatment is not racial discrimination if it is justified and proportionate. In Application of the Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide (Bosnia v. Serbia, ICJ 2007), the Court confirmed states' obligations under Genocide Convention distinct from CERD.

title: Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide 1948
source_name: Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide
text: The Genocide Convention, adopted three days after the UDHR on 9 December 1948, entered into force in 1951. Article 2 defines genocide as acts committed with intent to destroy, in whole or in part, a national, ethnical, racial or religious group. Prohibited acts include killing, causing serious bodily or mental harm, deliberately inflicting destructive conditions of life, imposing measures to prevent births, and forcibly transferring children. Article 1 establishes that genocide is a crime under international law whether committed in time of peace or war. States must prevent and punish genocide. The ICJ in Bosnia v. Serbia (2007) and Croatia v. Serbia (2015) confirmed the treaty's erga omnes and jus cogens character. The specific intent (dolus specialis) requirement was elaborated in Akayesu (ICTR 1998) and confirmed in Krstic (ICTY 2001) and Srebrenica proceedings.

title: UN Convention Relating to the Status of Refugees 1951
source_name: Refugee Convention and 1967 Protocol
text: The Refugee Convention defines who is a refugee (Article 1A(2): well-founded fear of persecution based on race, religion, nationality, political opinion or membership of a particular social group), the legal protection afforded to refugees, and States' obligations. Article 33 establishes the principle of non-refoulement: no expulsion or return to territories where a person's life or freedom would be threatened. The 1967 Protocol removed the geographic and temporal limitations. UNHCR, created in 1950, has the supervisory mandate. The UNHCR has given authoritative guidance on interpretation. Article 31 prohibits penalties for illegal entry by refugees directly arriving from persecution. The Convention does not apply to persons who have committed war crimes, crimes against humanity, or serious non-political crimes (Article 1F exclusion clauses). Non-refoulement under Article 33 has been held to constitute customary international law.

title: Convention Against Apartheid 1973
source_name: International Convention on the Suppression and Punishment of the Crime of Apartheid
text: The Apartheid Convention declares apartheid a crime against humanity and imposes on States Parties the obligation to suppress and punish the crime. Article 2 defines the crime of apartheid to include acts committed in the context of an institutionalised regime of domination and systematic oppression by one racial group over another racial group. This includes denial of life, liberty and security; imposing living conditions calculated to cause physical destruction of a group; legislative measures that divide the population along racial lines; and prohibition of mixed marriages. The crime of apartheid is codified as a crime against humanity in the Rome Statute (Article 7(1)(j)). The ICJ's 2024 advisory opinion on Legal Consequences Arising from the Policies and Practices of Israel in the Occupied Palestinian Territory applied these concepts.

title: UNCLOS 1982 — Key Provisions on Jurisdiction and Rights
source_name: United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea
text: UNCLOS, often called the 'constitution for the oceans', entered into force in 1994. Key jurisdictional zones: territorial sea (12 nautical miles, full sovereignty except innocent passage); contiguous zone (24 nm, limited control); exclusive economic zone (200 nm, sovereign rights over resources); continental shelf (up to 350 nm, rights over seabed resources). Article 87 establishes freedom of the high seas. Article 121 governs islands and their capacity to generate maritime zones (rocks that cannot sustain human habitation or economic life generate only territorial sea). ITLOS (International Tribunal for the Law of the Sea) adjudicates disputes. The Annex VII arbitral tribunal in South China Sea Arbitration (PCA 2016) ruled that China's historic rights claims within the nine-dash line were inconsistent with UNCLOS. Part XI was amended in 1994 to establish the International Seabed Authority.

title: New York Convention 1958 — Arbitration Enforcement
source_name: Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards
text: The New York Convention is the foundation of international commercial arbitration with 172 States Parties. Article II requires recognition of arbitration agreements and referral to arbitration unless the agreement is null and void. Article III requires recognition and enforcement of foreign arbitral awards. Article V provides exhaustive grounds for refusal: invalidity of the arbitration agreement; lack of notice or inability to present the case; award beyond scope; improper composition of tribunal; award not yet binding or set aside; subject matter not arbitrable; or enforcement contrary to public policy. Article VII preserves more favourable national law. The Convention applies to both commercial and non-commercial arbitration under the 1961 reservation option. National courts in most jurisdictions have adopted a pro-enforcement bias consistent with the Convention's object and purpose.

title: Paris Agreement on Climate Change 2015
source_name: Paris Agreement
text: The Paris Agreement entered into force in 2016 and has 195 Parties. Article 2 establishes the temperature goal: holding warming well below 2°C above pre-industrial levels and pursuing efforts to limit it to 1.5°C. Article 3 requires Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) through which Parties communicate and maintain progressive climate actions. Article 4 requires developed countries to take economy-wide emission reduction targets; developing countries are encouraged to move toward economy-wide targets over time. Article 6 allows cooperative approaches including carbon markets. Article 8 addresses loss and damage. Article 12 requires climate education and public awareness. The Agreement uses a 'pledge and review' model rather than binding emission targets. The ICJ's Advisory Opinion on climate change obligations was requested by the UN General Assembly in 2023 (proceedings ongoing). Climate change litigation has expanded significantly post-Paris, applying human rights frameworks in multiple jurisdictions.

title: Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities 2006
source_name: Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities
text: The CRPD, entered into force in 2008 with 186 States Parties, represents a paradigm shift from a medical model of disability to a social/human rights model. Article 3 establishes general principles including respect for inherent dignity, non-discrimination, full and effective participation, respect for difference, equality of opportunity, accessibility, equality between men and women. Article 12 guarantees equal recognition before the law and replaces substituted decision-making with supported decision-making for persons with disabilities. Article 13 requires access to justice on an equal basis. Article 19 protects the right to live independently and be included in the community. Article 24 guarantees inclusive education. Article 33 requires national implementation and monitoring frameworks. The CRPD Committee in General Comment 1 (2014) stated that guardianship laws that allow substituted decision-making are incompatible with Article 12.

title: Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties — Interpretation and Jus Cogens
source_name: Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties — Jus Cogens
text: The VCLT governs interpretation of treaties in Articles 31-33. Article 31: a treaty shall be interpreted in good faith in accordance with the ordinary meaning given to its terms in their context and in light of the object and purpose; subsequent agreements and subsequent practice shall also be taken into account. Article 32 permits recourse to supplementary means of interpretation including travaux préparatoires when the Article 31 interpretation leaves the meaning ambiguous, obscure or leads to a manifestly absurd result. Crucially, Articles 53 and 64 introduce the concept of jus cogens: peremptory norms of general international law from which no derogation is permitted. Article 53 voids treaties conflicting with existing jus cogens; Article 64 terminates treaties conflicting with new jus cogens. The ILC Conclusions on Peremptory Norms (2022) confirmed prohibitions of aggression, genocide, slavery, apartheid, torture, and the right of self-determination as jus cogens.

title: Rome Statute 1998 — Crimes Against Humanity
source_name: Rome Statute — Crimes Against Humanity
text: Rome Statute Article 7 defines crimes against humanity as acts committed as part of a widespread or systematic attack directed against any civilian population, with knowledge of the attack. Listed acts include murder; extermination; enslavement; deportation or forcible transfer; imprisonment in violation of fundamental rules of international law; torture; rape, sexual slavery, enforced prostitution, forced pregnancy, enforced sterilisation, or other forms of sexual violence; persecution against an identifiable group on political, racial, national, ethnic, cultural, religious, gender or other grounds; enforced disappearance; apartheid; and other inhumane acts. The contextual element (widespread or systematic, directed against any civilian population) distinguishes crimes against humanity from ordinary crimes. The ICC's jurisprudence has elaborated the 'knowledge of the attack' requirement as awareness that the conduct is part of a broader attack (Katanga judgment, 2014).

title: Human Rights Committee General Comment 36 — Right to Life
source_name: HRC General Comment No. 36 on Article 6 of the ICCPR: Right to Life
text: HRC General Comment 36 (2018) comprehensively reinterprets ICCPR Article 6 right to life. Key expansions: (1) the right to life extends beyond protection from arbitrary killing to encompass conditions necessary for a life with dignity, including rights to food, water, housing, health and a clean environment; (2) States must protect the right to life from threats by private actors including corporations; (3) the death penalty must comply with heightened procedural safeguards and the mandatory death penalty is incompatible with Article 6; (4) States' obligations apply extraterritorially when they exercise power or effective control over persons outside their territory; (5) climate change, natural disasters, and armed conflict implicate Article 6; (6) the right to life in the context of armed conflict is interpreted through IHL as lex specialis. The General Comment broke significant new ground on climate change and corporate accountability.

title: Optional Protocol to ICCPR — First Optional Protocol Individual Communications
source_name: Optional Protocol to the ICCPR
text: The First Optional Protocol to the ICCPR, adopted in 1966 alongside the Covenant and entered into force in 1976, allows individuals who claim to be victims of violations by a State Party to submit communications to the Human Rights Committee. 116 States Parties have recognised this jurisdiction. Requirements: the individual must have exhausted all available domestic remedies; the communication must not be anonymous; it must not be an abuse of the right of submission; it must not be under examination by another international procedure. The HRC issues 'Views' (not legally binding in international law but carrying significant moral and political weight). National courts in several jurisdictions (including Canada, the Netherlands) have treated HRC Views as having persuasive authority. The Second Optional Protocol (1989) abolishes the death penalty.

title: Barcelona Traction Light and Power Company Case — Erga Omnes Obligations
source_name: Barcelona Traction, Light and Power Company, Limited (Belgium v Spain)
text: In the Barcelona Traction case (ICJ 1970), the Court distinguished between obligations of a State towards the international community as a whole and those arising vis-à-vis another State. Obligations erga omnes derive from the outlawing of acts of aggression and genocide, and from the principles and rules concerning the basic rights of the human person, including protection from slavery and racial discrimination. The Court held at paragraphs 33-34: 'by their very nature the former are the concern of all States. In view of the importance of the rights involved, all States can be held to have a legal interest in their protection; they are obligations erga omnes.' The concept has been developed further in the Wall Advisory Opinion (2004), Application of the Genocide Convention cases, and the ILC Articles on State Responsibility (Articles 41 and 48) which allow any State to invoke responsibility for breaches of obligations erga omnes.

title: Case Concerning the Legal Consequences of the Construction of a Wall — ICJ 2004
source_name: Legal Consequences of the Construction of a Wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory, Advisory Opinion
text: The ICJ's 2004 Wall Advisory Opinion addressed the legality of Israel's construction of a wall in the Occupied Palestinian Territory. Key holdings: (1) GCIV applies to the OPT; (2) the route of the wall through OPT violates GCIV Article 49 and customary IHL; (3) ICCPR, ICESCR, and CRC apply extraterritorially in territories under a State's effective control; (4) the right of the Palestinian people to self-determination is an erga omnes obligation; (5) all States are obliged not to recognise the illegal situation created by the wall and not to render aid or assistance in maintaining the situation. This opinion expanded the erga omnes doctrine to include the right to self-determination and confirmed the extraterritorial application of human rights treaties alongside IHL in situations of occupation.

title: ILC Articles on State Responsibility — Erga Omnes and Countermeasures
source_name: ILC Articles on Responsibility of States for Internationally Wrongful Acts
text: The ILC Articles on State Responsibility (2001), endorsed by UNGA Resolution 56/83, codify secondary rules of international law governing when a State bears responsibility for internationally wrongful acts. Articles 40-41 address serious breaches of peremptory norms (jus cogens), requiring all States to cooperate to bring an end to the breach through lawful means and not recognise the situation as lawful. Article 48 allows any State, not just injured States, to invoke responsibility for breaches of obligations erga omnes. The injured State under Article 42 may resort to countermeasures under Articles 49-54. The Commentary to Article 48 confirms that erga omnes obligations include the prohibition of aggression, genocide, slavery, and apartheid; fundamental humanitarian obligations; and the right of self-determination. The Articles have been widely applied by international courts and tribunals, and parts have been recognised as reflecting customary international law.

title: Vienna Convention on Consular Relations 1963
source_name: Vienna Convention on Consular Relations
text: The VCCR governs consular relations between States, protecting consular officers and functions. Article 36 provides the right of consular notification: when nationals of the sending State are arrested, detained or committed to prison, the competent authorities must inform the individual without delay of their right to have the consulate notified; if the individual so requests, the consulate must be notified. This provision has been the subject of extensive ICJ litigation: LaGrand (Germany v. USA, 2001) confirmed Article 36 creates individual rights; Avena (Mexico v. USA, 2004) required review and reconsideration of death sentences of Mexican nationals; Jadhav (India v. Pakistan, 2019) confirmed Article 36 rights were violated by Pakistan. Article 41 grants consular officers personal inviolability (different from the complete immunity of diplomatic agents).

title: Second Optional Protocol to ICCPR — Abolition of Death Penalty
source_name: Second Optional Protocol to the ICCPR aiming at the abolition of the death penalty
text: The Second Optional Protocol to the ICCPR, entered into force in 1991 with 90 States Parties, obligates States to abolish the death penalty. Article 1 provides that no one within the jurisdiction of a State Party shall be executed. No reservations are permitted except for wartime application for serious military crimes. As of 2024, 112 countries have abolished the death penalty in law and 144 in law or practice. The Human Rights Committee has held in General Comment 36 (2018) that the death penalty is only permitted in the most serious crimes (crimes with lethal or extremely grave outcome) and that mandatory death penalty violates Article 6. The ECOSOC Safeguards Guaranteeing Protection of the Rights of Those Facing the Death Penalty (1984) and the UN Commission on Human Rights resolutions have progressively narrowed the scope of permissible application.
